data.title: Macron: Trump wants to 'weaken and subordinate' Europe
data.description:
French President Emmanuel Macron on Tuesday accused President Trump of wanting to “weaken and subordinate” Europe. 

Macron made the comments in front of global leaders at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland. The remarks came amid a furor over Trump’s push to seize Greenland and his vow to place tariffs on imports from Denmark and seven other European Union allies who have rallied around Denmark, a NATO ally that controls Greenland.

“It’s clear that we are reaching a time of instability, of imbalances, both from the security and defense point of view and from the economic point of view,” Macron said.

Macron called the present moment “a very concerning time” in international cooperation. 

“Competition from the United States of America through trade agreements that undermine our export interests, demand maximum concessions and openly aim to weaken and subordinate Europe, combined with an endless accumulation of new tariffs that are fundamentally unacceptable, even more so when they are used so as leverage against territorial sovereignty,” Macron said. 

The remarks also came after Trump posted screenshots on Truth Social early Tuesday morning of messages Macron and other foreign leaders had sent him.

The Macron text chain shows the French president telling Trump, “I do not understand what you are doing on Greenland” and offering to set up a G7 meeting in Paris on Thursday. The U.S. president is set to sign the charter for his “Board of Peace” on Thursday morning. 

Additionally, after Macron said he was not ready yet to accept an invitation to join Trump’s board, the American president threatened to impose a “200% tariff” on French Champagne and wine. 

France has already been included in the Trump administration’s retaliatory tariffs against European countries who opposed his bid to acquire Greenland. The U.S. plans to impose a 10% import tax on all goods from France, Denmark, Sweden, Norway, Germany, the United Kingdom and the Netherlands. 

In his speech in Davos on Tuesday, Macron also appeared to mock Trump’s claims that he has solved eight wars during his time in office. 

“Look at the situation, where we are, I mean, a shift towards autocracy against democracy, more violence, more than 60 wars in 2024, an absolute record, even if I understood a few of them were fixed, and — and conflict has become normalized, hybrid, expanding into new demands — space, digital, information, cyber, trade and so on,” Macron said.

data.title: EU chief: Proposed Trump tariffs over Greenland a 'mistake'
data.description:
Ursula von der Leyen, the president of the European Commission, criticized President Trump for proposing tariffs on European Union (EU) nations over their support of Greenland’s sovereignty.

“Artic security can only be achieved together, and this is why the proposed additional tariffs are a mistake, especially between long-standing allies,” von der Leyen said Tuesday at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland. “The European Union and the United States have agreed to a trade deal last July. 

“And in politics, as in business, a deal is a deal. And when friends shake hands, it must mean something.”

Trump, who will head to Switzerland for the summit Tuesday evening, announced Saturday that imports from Denmark, Norway, Sweden, France, Germany, United Kingdom, the Netherlands and Finland will be subject to 10 percent tariffs starting Feb. 1. The levies will increase to 25 percent on June 1.

The president added that the import taxes will be in place until Denmark and Greenland agree to a deal that grants the U.S. control of the mineral-rich island. 

“We have subsidized Denmark, and all of the Countries of the European Union, and others, for many years by not charging them tariffs, or any other forms of remuneration,” Trump said. “Now, after centuries, it is time for Denmark to give back — World Peace is at stake!”

Von der Leyen and Trump previously reached a trade deal between the EU and U.S. last July. Under the agreement, European imports into the U.S. faced 15 percent tariffs, while the EU agreed to purchase $750 billion worth of energy from the U.S. and invest an additional $600 billion in the U.S. for other goods. 

“I think it’s the biggest deal ever made,” the president claimed at the time. Von der Leyen, meanwhile, said the deal would “bring stability, it will bring predictability.”

European leaders, including von der Leyen, pushed back Saturday against Trump’s proposed levies. The European Commission president wrote on the social platform X the tariffs “would undermine transatlantic relations and risk a dangerous downward spiral.” 

“Europe will remain united, coordinated, and committed to upholding its sovereignty,” she added.

Days before the president unveiled the new import taxes, European nations sent troops to Greenland as part of a joint exercise led by the Danish military. The buildup comes after Denmark boosted its military presence on the island last summer in an effort to strengthen Europe’s “footprint” in the Arctic.

data.title: What's behind Trump's delay on attacking Iran?
data.description:
Just over two weeks ago, in a “one-day war,” the U.S. launched a mission that seized Venezuelan leader Nicolas Maduro and his wife. As of a few days ago, in response to President Trump’s threat to acquire Greenland, several NATO allies deployed a small number of forces to prevent its NATO partner from invading a fellow NATO partner.  

Given the bizarre nature of these events, it’s hard to believe they actually happened.

What hasn’t happened is another question. Why has the U.S. not yet attacked Iran to protect the Iranians rioting in protest against an incompetent and autocratic government? Let’s pose an entirely speculative thought experiment to address that question.  

First, press reports have been vague as to why no strike has occurred. Trump was adamant. Many, myself included, believed that when the U.S. had sufficient forces in the region, an attack would be launched. For the time being, Trump has backed off that threat arguing that the mullahs in Tehran are no longer killing their citizens protesting their rule. 

Whether that assessment is correct or not, it makes sense that a president would declare victory if they believed the killing had stopped. But let us speculate a bit more.

No doubt Trump was serious in making that threat. With the success of Maduro’s capture behind him and the assurance of America’s military prowess in the Midnight Hammer missions last June that hammered Iran’s nuclear infrastructure, taking on the ayatollahs would seem easy. But what might his advisors cautioned?

Before Midnight Hammer and in his first term, Trump halted a retaliatory hit against Iran after it had shot down a U.S. Reaper spy drone. Why? The reason given was the possibility of collateral civilian casualties. Would that be repeated if the U.S. struck a range of Iranian targets?

Someone might have briefed the president on Operation Eagle Claw, the failed 1980 hostage rescue mission to free U.S. citizens held in the former U.S. embassy in Tehran. That fiasco ended in disaster almost before it started, with a collision between one of the mission’s CH-46 helicopters and a C-130 refueled at Desert One in a remote part of Iran. 

Operation Eagle Claw, in part, cost Jimmy Carter the 1980 election. With U.S. elections in November, would Trump risk another failure? While there were not sufficient forces in the Gulf to assure a successful strike to halt the violence, no doubt planning was completed on what to hit and what not.  

One can assume that cyberattacks to immobilize Iranian defenses, much as in Venezuela, would be carried out. Attempts to restore the internet or not would be considered. And no doubt greater sanctions against the mullahs and the Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps would be imposed. But what other targets might be struck?

The fundamental question must be: What is the strategic purpose of any action in Iran, kinetic or otherwise? Is it to prevent the further slaughter of Iranians? Is it to foment a regime change? Is it to punish the theocracy? Or was it to force the mullahs from further violence against the public?

Given the broad nature of the riots, preventing further violence would require striking many targets, presumably of the Revolutionary Guard Corps and its Basij units. Although the Quds force primarily acts abroad to support allies, it might be called into action against civilians. So it is unclear how, other than punitive strikes, a sufficient number of regime elements could be targeted to stop violence against protesting Iranians.

On regime change, who would govern once the mullahs were out? There is no sign of an internal government that is ready to take charge. If external leaders such as Crown Prince Reza Pahlavi, the shah’s son, were to return — as was tried in Iraq — the chances of success would be slim. And the Revolutionary Guard Corps might be even more radical than the clerics in putting down any protests and even restarting the nuclear program. And punishing the regime made little sense.

So what is the administration’s Iran policy today? While awaiting the arrival of a carrier strike group to the Gulf, here is a radical idea. Why not negotiate with the mullahs to determine if the regime will be prepared to allow more freedoms to its people? Can the nuclear agreement be restarted? And can Iran return to the international community? 

Perhaps the answers are no. But why not try?

Harlan Ullman, Ph.D., is UPI’s Arnaud deBorchgrave Distinguished Columnist, a senior advisor at the Atlantic Council, the chairman of two private companies and the principal author of the doctrine of shock and awe. He and former United Kingdom Defense Chief David Richards are the authors of a forthcoming book on preventing strategic catastrophe.

data.title: House panel opens Minnesota fraud investigation
data.description:
The House Energy and Commerce Committee announced Tuesday it has opened a social services fraud investigation into the state of Minnesota.

“The extensive fraud schemes being perpetrated in Minnesota have wreaked havoc on government-funded health programs. We have an obligation to ensure finite taxpayer dollars are being used responsibly, and that the most vulnerable Americans are not being exploited to the benefit of fraudsters and foreign actors,” Committee Chair Brett Guthrie (R-Ky.) and GOP Reps. John Joyce (Pa.) and Morgan Griffith (Va.) said in a statement announcing the investigation.

“As members of Congress and this Committee, our track record has made our continued commitment to ridding government programs of waste, fraud, mismanagement, and abuse clear. This letter is the next step in the Committee’s work to root out fraud and restore program integrity in our federal health programs nationwide,” they added. 

A source told The Hill’s sister channel NewsNation that the probe will focus on state-administered Medicaid programs in an “industrial scale” investigation.

Close

Thank you for signing up!

Subscribe to more newsletters here

The latest in politics and policy. Direct to your inbox. Sign up for the Morning Report newsletter

The GOP-led panel plans to push Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) to provide by the end of the month details on all audits that relate to 14 social services programs that the state previously deemed to be prone to “high-risk provider types,” Fox News Digital first reported.

Lawmakers are demanding documents, audits and records dating back to 2019 seeking answers on whether state officials should have been able to recognize, catch or prevent fraudulent schemes.

“Ensuring Medicaid program integrity is critical to preserving access to vital health care services for those that need it most,” the GOP lawmakers said in a letter to Walz.

“Every dollar stolen from the Medicaid program by fraudsters is taken from children, pregnant women, the elderly, and people with disabilities,” it added.

The Minnesota governor will also be required to produce all communications with state Medicaid Commissioner John Connolly.

“Many of Minnesota’s Medicaid programs were designed in such a way that made them
vulnerable to fraud. The Housing Stabilization Support (HSS) program was established to “help people with disabilities and seniors find and keep housing in their own home,'” the letter states.

“Instead of directly providing housing to those in need, HSS paid consultants to help people find housing, attracting fraudsters from all over the U. S. to register as HSS providers with low barriers to entry and little oversight over reimbursements,” it added.

Guthrie alleged Walz’s administration “willfully ignored” whistleblower reports, further preventing the fraud from being uncovered.

“If Minnesota fails to comply with federal Medicaid requirements, CMS [Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services] will begin withholding Medicaid funds from the state until it comes into compliance and implements a corrective action plan to address rampant FWA [fraud, waste and abuse],” the letter states.

The Hill has reached out Walz’s office for comment.

The committee investigation follows the departure of Joseph H. Thompson, who resigned from his position as the First Assistant U.S. Attorney in the U.S. Attorney’s Office for the District of Minnesota after being tasked with overseeing the federal investigation into allegations that social services programs in the state defrauded the federal government of millions of dollars.

Walz, who has dropped his reelection bid amid the fraud controversy in Minnesota, called the prosecutor’s departure “a huge loss for our state” and alleged it was the latest instance of the administration “pushing nonpartisan career professionals out of the Justice Department, replacing them with his sycophants.”

data.title: Trump on Norway denying Nobel prize control: 'I don't care'
data.description:
President Trump insisted Monday that he does not care about the Nobel Peace Prize but maintained that Norway “controlled” the award despite the country’s leaders denying any involvement in deciding who receives the prize.

“I don’t care about the Nobel Prize,” Trump told reporters.

“First of all, a very fine woman felt that I deserved it and really wanted me to have the Nobel Prize and I appreciate that,” he added, referring to Venezuelan opposition leader María Corina Machado’s decision to give her 18-carat gold medal to Trump during a meeting at the White House last week.

That move, though not mentioned directly, prompted the Nobel Committee to note that the while the symbols of the prize, a medal and diploma, can be given away the honor itself is “inseparably linked” to the winner.  

Trump’s comments came days after he linked the prize — which he has long coveted — to his aspirations to acquire Greenland in a letter to Norwegian Prime Minster Jonas Gahr Støre, writing that he no longer felt “an obligation to think purely of Peace.”

“Dear Jonas: Considering your Country decided not to give me the Nobel Peace Prize for having stopped 8 Wars PLUS, I no longer feel an obligation to think purely of Peace, although it will always be predominant, but can now think about what is good and proper for the United States of America,” the president wrote, according to The Hill’s sister station, News Nation.

Støre clarified in a statement on Monday that the Norwegian government holds no sway over who is receives the Peace Prize.  

“As regards the Nobel Peace Prize, I have clearly explained, including to President Trump what is well known, the prize is awarded by an independent Nobel Committee and not the Norwegian Government,’ the prime minister wrote.

Speaking to reporters on Monday night, Trump rejected that notion.  

“If anybody thinks that Norway doesn’t – doesn’t control the Nobel Prize, they’re just kidding,” he said. “They have a board, but it’s controlled by Norway. And I don’t care what Norway said.”

The president spent months publicly lobbying to win the Peace Prize before it was awarded to Machado, often claiming he deserved it because he had helped to end several wars.

“But I really don’t care about [the Nobel Prize],” Trump insisted on Monday. “What I care about is saving lives. And I think I’ve saved tens of millions of lives…we stopped eight wars and maybe we’ll be stopping a ninth very soon, we’ll see.”

The Trump administration has ratcheted up the pressure on European countries in recent weeks as the president eyes a U.S. takeover of Greenland, a self-governing Danish territory. He announced Saturday that he would implement a 10 percent tariff on Demark and its allies starting Feb. 1, and potentially raising to 25 percent in June, until a deal is reached.

The president and other senior U.S. officials have argued that the island must come under U.S. ownership to protect national security interests and thwart threats from Russia and China in the Arctic region.

Trump has not ruled out the possibility of using military force, despite strong opposition from Americans and warnings that such action could mean the end the longstanding NATO alliance.

data.title: Watch live: World Economic Forum in Davos
data.description:
World leaders are meeting in Davos, Switzerland this week as part of the World Economic Forum to discuss geopolitics, tech, trade and more. 

President Trump is set to address the forum on Wednesday amid tensions with European allies over his push for the U.S. to acquire Greenland, the semiautonomous arctic territory within the Kingdom of Denmark. 

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, who spoke at the forum on Tuesday, warned European leaders against imposing retaliatory tariffs after Trump threatened a 10 percent import tax starting Feb. 1 on Denmark and its allies who do not support the U.S. taking over Greenland.

The president is expected to meet with NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte and EU allies during his trip.

Watch the live feed above.

data.title: To restore trust in public health, Robert F. Kennedy Jr. must go
data.description:
Earlier this month, Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. announced that the U.S. will stop recommending several childhood vaccines, ignoring the guidance from scientific and healthcare experts. 

In a statement, the department nonsensically claimed that one reason for doing so is that “loss of trust during the pandemic not only affected the COVID-19 vaccine uptake. It also contributed to less adherence to the full [Centers for Disease Control and Prevention] childhood immunization schedule.” 

Despite Kennedy’s repeated assertions that his goal is to “restore trust” in public health, he is actually destroying it. 

First, let’s remember that he has no training or experience in public health, science or medicine; he is an attorney and activist who promotes anti-science, anti-vaccine conspiracy theories. Despite these dubious credentials, he maintains a grandiose belief that only he has the knowledge and insight to restore faith in public health, disdaining hundreds of years of science and medical discovery, and those with decades of experience in the field.  

As a result, public health experts have been sidelined, demonized, mocked, fired and silenced by Kennedy and his political appointees as he campaigns against the safety and efficacy of vaccines. 

So, has this strategy achieved Kennedy’s stated goal? Our look into recent data shows that Kennedy has, in reality, increased distrust. 

One indicator is confidence in the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, which 66 percent of Americans trusted in December of 2024. The level of trust had dropped to 60 percent by June 2025, four months after Kennedy’s confirmation. By October, trust had decreased further to 54 percent, according to the Axios-Ipsos American Health Index poll. 

Uncertainty about the reliability of the Food and Drug Administration mirrors this finding. Similar results were reported in a poll conducted by the University of Pennsylvania in August. Correspondingly, most professional medical organizations now warn against believing information from the Kennedy-run CDC, and Canadian officials have correctly stated in no uncertain terms that “U.S. health and science institutions can no longer be depended upon for accurate information, particularly when it comes to vaccinations.”

For decades, Kennedy has repeatedly attacked the safety and efficacy of vaccines, especially the measles, mumps and rubella vaccine, and continues to do so in his current role as secretary. The impact? Even though these interventions were proven safe and effective long ago, confidence in vaccines continues to plummet under his leadership.

A Pew Research Center poll published in November showed that in 2023, approximately 86 percent of Republicans said that the benefits of the measles, mumps and rubella vaccine outweigh the risks. By last October, this figure had dropped to 78 percent. These data suggest that Kennedy’s vaccine policies are eroding confidence in public health overall among both Republicans and Democrats. 

Since Kennedy took office as HHS secretary, the U.S. has seen the worst measles outbreak in nire than 20 years. Consequently, the nation may soon lose its measles elimination status. We are also seeing one of the worst whooping cough outbreaks in years, as well as one of the worst flu seasons in years.  

Despite all this, there has been little response from Kennedy about the outbreaks, no recommendation for vaccinations or masking and no acknowledgement of the growing crisis. What has occurred are mass firings of public health experts, replacement of vaccine advisory committee members with anti-vaccine advocates and scaled-back communicable disease surveillance. 

This is accompanied by a constant drumbeat of disinformation from Kennedy and his acolytes that he has installed in key roles. Kennedy unilaterally ordered changes to the CDC website, rejecting the evidence-based understanding of public health shared by most CDC experts. 

Restoring faith in public health is clearly not one of Kennedy’s goals, nor is it within his skillset. In order to do that, Kennedy must be removed from office. Only one in three Americans believe that Kennedy is a source of trustworthy information about vaccines, and 59 percent disapprove of his job performance, according to a Kaiser Family Foundation tracking poll published in October.  

Americans have spoken. Kennedy must go.   

Elizabeth Jacobs is an epidemiologist and professor emerita at the University of Arizona. She is a founding member of the advocacy group Defend Public Health. James Alwine is a virologist, a professor emeritus at the University of Pennsylvania, a visiting professor at the University of Arizona and a fellow of the American Academy for Microbiology and of the American Association for the Advancement of Science. He is a member of the coordinating committee for the advocacy group Defend Public Health.

data.title: Why should Democrats trust George Conway now?
data.description:
George Conway likes to say that he never expected to run for Congress. He claims, if asked years ago, he would have laughed at the idea. And yet, this month, Conway officially threw his hat into the ring, announcing a bid to represent New York’s 12th congressional district as a Democrat.  

The move was framed not as ambition but as obligation. According to Conway, the moment has arrived when lawyers like him are uniquely needed to confront Donald Trump and restore accountability to American government. 

It is a dramatic framing. It also relies on selective memory. 

Conway, a former Republican power-lawyer once married to Kellyanne Conway, did not emerge from outside the political system. He was part of it. He celebrated Trump’s election in 2016. He moved comfortably within conservative legal circles that helped legitimize Trump’s rise. And when the Trump presidency devolved into chaos, grievance and spectacle, Conway did not step away from the stage. He became a familiar presence on it. 

For years, the Conways occupied a peculiar and highly visible role in American political culture — Kellyanne inside the White House and George outside, publicly attacking the president while benefiting from the same media ecosystem that rewarded constant conflict. Their marriage and their dueling cable appearances became part of the Trump-era theater. It generated attention, relevance and social capital. It did not meaningfully restrain the administration or mitigate the damage it caused.

There is also a basic political reality that Conway’s candidacy must confront: It is difficult to argue he has ever been broadly appealing to large swaths of voters. His public persona has long been caustic and combative, better suited to legal briefs and cable panels than to coalition-building. The media appearances surrounding his announcement suggest that little has changed. Confrontation may play well on television, but Congress is not a courtroom, and governance is not persuasion by insult. 

Supporters might argue that likability is overrated, that this is a moment for blunt truth-tellers unconcerned with tone. But legislation is not passed by monologues. It requires trust, negotiation and relationships built over time. A temperament forged for perpetual opposition is not obviously equipped for that work, particularly in a closely divided Congress where progress often depends on the unglamorous art of compromise. 

Now Conway argues that commentary is no longer enough. That this moment demands elected power. That what once could be handled through legal analysis and public critique must now be addressed from inside Congress. 

But Conway has been shaping political narratives for nearly a decade without holding office. He has enjoyed influence without accountability, visibility without responsibility, and relevance without having to answer to voters. Running for Congress is not a departure from politics. It is simply a new chapter in a long political career that has always hovered close to power while avoiding its burdens. 

The choice of venue is telling. New York’s 12th congressional district is among the safest Democratic seats in the country. With Rep. Jerry Nadler (D) retiring, the primary field is crowded, competitive and firmly anti-Trump by default. Conway is not testing his appeal in hostile territory. He is entering a district where opposition to Trump is already consensus, not courage. 

The Democratic primary includes candidates with strong community ties, legislative experience, and longstanding relationships with voters. They may differ in ideology and style, but none must explain a late-stage ideological migration that conveniently ends in the bluest possible landing spot. 

That is why skepticism is warranted. Conway presents himself as a reluctant outsider compelled to act. In reality, he is a seasoned insider seeking a new role in a familiar ecosystem. His public record suggests a comfort with conflict more than construction, with performance more than governance.

There is also a broader lesson here for Democrats. Opposition to Trump, however justified, is not a governing philosophy. Being loudly against something does not automatically translate into being effective at building something. The work of Congress requires patience, discipline, and a willingness to operate beyond the spotlight. Those qualities have never been central to Conway’s public brand. 

Republicans have learned, at great cost, what happens when personality and spectacle are mistaken for leadership. Democrats should be careful not to repeat that mistake by rewarding familiarity and media fluency over consistency and substance. 

Conway may sincerely believe he is answering a call. But voters are entitled to ask harder questions. Why now? Why in this district? And why should someone who helped normalize the culture of political spectacle be trusted to help dismantle it?

Democracy does not need another protagonist from the Trump era. It needs leaders who never treated that era as entertainment in the first place. 

Aron Solomon is chief strategy officer for AMPLIFY and has taught entrepreneurship at McGill University and the University of Pennsylvania.

data.title: Trump announces signing ceremony Thursday for Gaza peace board
data.description:
A signing ceremony to formalize President Trump’s Board of Peace will be held Thursday at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland. 

Trump is set to meet with global leaders later this week and will sign the Board of Peace charter on Thursday morning, according to a White House schedule. 

The board, headed by Trump, will oversee the next phase of his administration’s 20-point peace plan and oversee the reconstruction of postwar Gaza. 

Trump extended board membership offers to several heads of state, including Russian President Vladimir Putin. Leaders of the United Kingdom, Israel, Canada, France, Brazil, Egypt, Turkey and Argentina also received invitations to join the group. It’s unclear how many countries were offered membership. 

The inclusion of Israel in the board’s invitation list has been particularly controversial, as the country would be working on a ceasefire agreement that directly involves them. 

Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has yet to publicly accept Trump’s membership offer and it’s unclear whether he plans to join the board. The Israeli head of state has previously said the body was out of step with Israel and “is contrary to its policy.”

France doesn’t intend to join the board “at this stage,” according to an official close to French President Emmanuel Macron. 

Trump brushed off this news on Monday, telling reporters, “nobody wants him anyway because he’s going to be out of office very soon.” He threatened to impose a “200% tariff” on French wines and champagne, arguing that Macron would then accept the invitation.

“But he doesn’t have to join,” he added. 

Other executive board members include Secretary of State Marco Rubio, special envoy Steve Witkoff, Trump’s son-in-law, Jared Kushner, former U.K. Prime Minister Tony Blair, World Bank President Ajay Banga and billionaire investors Robert Gabriel and Marc Rowan, the White House announced last Friday. 

The U.S. president has reportedly tied a $1 billion membership fee to all countries who sign on to join the board permanently, with funds supporting rebuilding efforts in the Gaza Strip. Three-year appointments do not carry this required fee, a U.S. official with knowledge of the charter told The Associated Press.

data.title: Newsom on world leaders' response to Trump: 'Pathetic'
data.description:
California Gov. Gavin Newsom (D) on Tuesday slammed world leaders, calling their responses to President Trump’s foreign policy initiatives “pathetic.”

“Do you have a message for Europeans who are concerned about the messages from the White House around Greenland this week?” Sky News’s Paul Kelso asked Newsom in Davos, Switzerland.

“Yeah, it’s time to buck up,” Newsom replied. “It’s time to get serious. And stop being complicit. It’s time to stand tall and firm, have a backbone. You know, I’ve seen this in the United States, supine Congress, playing both sides. You know, say one thing on a text or a tweet, another publicly.”

Newsom added later that he “can’t take this complicity” and that world leaders’ interactions with the president have been “pathetic.”

The California governor’s comments come in the wake of controversial foreign policy moves by the Trump administration, such as a push to acquire Greenland and the capture of Venezuelan leader Nicolás Maduro. 

Trump on Monday did not dismiss using force to take over Greenland, which is a Danish territory, NBC News reported. The president has had his sights on the Arctic island for a while, but has ramped up his threats of takeover in recent weeks.

Newsom, seen as a possible 2028 Democratic presidential candidate, has been notably pushing hard against the president. The California governor’s press office often mocks Trump on social media, using a posting style similar to the president’s that features all caps words and brash statements.

Newsom also posted a clip of his Tuesday comments on the social platform X, saying in the post that “America’s allies and business leaders need to understand this: There’s no diplomacy with Donald Trump.”

“Get off your knees and grow a spine,” he added.

The Hill has reached out to the White House for comment on Newsom’s remarks.
